executive_briefing.summary: Liberation Day tariff shock activates Cascade Protocol; global stress regime escalates to CRITICAL; EM Equities collapse to BEARISH; Bonds, Metals flip BEARISH — 6 of 8 asset classes now BEARISH
executive_briefing.one_number_to_watch: CME futures: 60% probability no Fed cuts through end-2026. If that number falls below 40% (cut expectations return), it means the market has priced in a hard landing and expects emergency easing — the Fast Cascade scenario is underway.
executive_briefing.one_indicator_lying: Earnings revisions (+12.5% Q1 est.) remain GREEN with Blind Spot Rule 1 active. These figures pre-date tariff impact on corporate guidance. Q1 earnings season begins this week — watch for downside guidance from export-exposed sectors as the first real read-through of Liberation Day.

hard_landing_risk.note: Liberation Day tariff shock confirmed worst case; EM Equities collapsing (MILD NEGATIVE → BEARISH); energy shock inflation not abating; Fed higher-for-longer; hard landing probability materially above Issue 2 baseline of 0.38

flags.title: Hyperscaler capex trajectory reinforces AI Infrastructure Debt indicator
flags.body: MAINTAINED: OpenAI $122B total funding at $852B valuation. Tariff shock applies directly to semiconductor supply chains and AI hardware imports — tariff cost on AI infrastructure capex is now a confirmed headwind. Data centre electricity cost inflation from energy shock erodes AI capex ROI. Private credit gate events undermine the cheap capital assumption behind AI capex funding models. AI capex remains both a debt amplifier (2026-2027 risk) and potential deflationary force (2028-2030).

flags.title: AI labour displacement beginning to show in entry-level employment data
flags.body: MAINTAINED: NBER Working Paper 34836: 502,000 AI-driven job cuts projected in 2026 — 9x the 55,000 in 2025. Critical dynamic: if realised, would push jobless_claims and consumer_confidence both RED, accelerating Consumer Staples BEARISH score. Fed cannot model AI displacement masked beneath aggregate labour statistics.

flags.title: EU CSRD Phase 2 creates mandatory climate risk disclosure for financial institutions
flags.body: MAINTAINED: CSRD Phase 2 in force since 1 January 2026; first reporting obligations 2027. Double materiality requirements expose EU financial institutions. Structural cost amplifier for G-SIB institutions already under capital pressure. Liberation Day tariffs add a parallel structural cost via supply chain compliance requirements.

flags.title: Iran/Hormuz conflict drives largest oil supply disruption in history — directly drives oil_supply_shock RED escalation
flags.body: MAINTAINED: Hormuz flows at ~4M bpd (vs normal 16M bpd). No AIS-confirmed commercial resumption. 5yr breakeven inflation +26bps since conflict onset — the energy shock is now directly embedded in CB policy. Sudan economic warfare and EM sovereign distress compounding. This is the dominant near-term driver of the GMM Energy score (-0.865 BEARISH) and key input into Consumer Staples and EM Equities.

flags.title: No material macro-financial cross-signal identified this period
flags.body: No FIMI-driven market manipulation identified. Hungary election resolved April 12 — outcome now known (watch for HUF and Hungarian sovereign spread reaction to post-election government formation).

flags.title: Hungary election April 12 — HUF and sovereign spread stress potential; tariff coercion as WDM-GMM structural link
flags.body: UPDATED: Hungary election April 12 is 5 days away. Tisza-Fidesz race remains tight. Macro relevance: Tisza victory + Russian economic retaliation scenario could spike HUF and Hungarian sovereign spreads. Additional WDM-GMM structural link: Liberation Day tariff package is politically driven economic coercion — WDM cms-005 flag (economic coercion as democratic erosion mechanism) is now confirmed at institutional level.

flags.title: US tariff shock (EU 20%) directly targets European economies — upgraded from NoSignal to Active
flags.body: UPGRADED: Liberation Day tariffs at 20% on EU are a direct economic coercion event — ESA cross-monitor relevance confirmed. ECB citing material near-term inflation from energy shock; EU Anti-Coercion Instrument filings initiated. Transatlantic decoupling protocol activated. Financial contagion from Treasury basis trade dislocation would affect ECB collateral availability. US-EU trade relationship is in structural transition — ESA designation upgraded from NoSignal to Active.

weekly_brief:
## Lead Signal

ECB President Lagarde warned that the Strait of Hormuz closure represents the largest oil supply disruption in history, framing the current period as a relentless shock cascade encompassing pandemic, land war, energy crisis, sweeping tariffs since the 1930s, and now this Hormuz event. This energy shock compounds the April 2 Liberation Day tariff package, forging dual supply-side shocks that lock in stagflation risk. ECB baseline projections assume contained conflict, yet adverse scenarios indicate prolonged disruption would significantly reduce growth and elevate inflation beyond baseline levels. The macro health composite score stands at 0.32, deteriorating under these pressures, with indicator domains flashing red across growth recession and inflation central bank categories.

Euro area headline inflation rose to 2.6 percent in March from 1.9 percent in February, driven by an energy inflation spike, while ECB projects 2.6 percent inflation for 2026, up from December forecasts due to energy price increases. Core inflation eased slightly to 2.3 percent, and wage growth slowed to 3.7 percent in Q4 2025, with underlying indicators assessed consistent with the 2 percent medium-term target but carrying upside risk from the energy shock. This configuration signals entrenched inflationary pressures amid growth headwinds, as dual shocks from tariffs and energy create a stagflationary environment locking in downturn beyond typical cyclical patterns.

## Other Developments

**Commodity Price Transmission escalates to HIGH.** ECB characterized the Strait of Hormuz closure as the largest oil supply disruption in history, with the energy shock compounding tariff cascade to create dual supply-side shocks locking stagflation risk. This vector rating changed this week, reflecting intensified transmission from geopolitical disruption to macroeconomic stress.

**EU-US Turnberry tariff deal locks 15 percent rate.** The deal secures a 15 percent tariff rate on EU exports, reducing uncertainty but eroding competitiveness as tariffs on rivals converge to similar levels. EU steel and aluminum face sustained 25 percent and 10 percent US Section 232 tariffs, imposing high costs on auto, machinery, and construction exporters. USTR 2026 Trade Policy Agenda doubles down on America First tariffs, enforcing existing measures and monitoring commitments, with tariff escalation rung stable at 3 but risks to rung 4 via USMCA review.

**BIS warns crypto exchanges function as shadow banks.** BIS characterized major platforms including Binance, Coinbase, Bybit, OKX, Crypto.com, and MEXC as a shadow crypto financial system engaging in risk transformation, exposing users to credit, liquidity, and maturity risks without prudential safeguards. This assessment highlights systemic vulnerabilities in the private credit cascade vector rated MODERATE.

**ECB enhances EUREP liquidity facility amid volatility.** ECB introduced standing access for all central banks unless excluded for AML or sanctions reasons, reflecting a more uncertain and potentially volatile global environment with frequent financial disruptions. This move addresses liquidity fragmentation and currency regime stress, both rated ELEVATED, and speeds up liquidity provision in the financial stability domain rated Amber and worsening.

**G-7 central banks coordinate rate holds.** G-7 central banks held rates this week, watching energy costs and inflation risks, with policy rate divergence rated ELEVATED. Federal Reserve forward guidance notes tariff and energy uncertainty, while ECB held rates in March amid energy shock.

## Cross-Monitor Connections

This week macro signals link to ESA via Turnberry deal and Parliament vote locking US-EU tariff friction as economic coercion, with ECB energy shock elevating EUR growth-inflation divergence. ERM connects through Hormuz closure as largest oil shock plus Section 232 steel and aluminum tariffs signaling energy and metals supply chain stress in commodity price transmission. SCEM ties emerge from USTR agenda enforcement and EM exporter tariff convergence risking sovereign stress spillover to conflict financing.

## Outlook

Next week watch national ratification status of EU-US trade deal, as pending approvals could destabilize tariff rung 3 or trigger escalation to rung 4. USMCA Joint Review progress will clarify escalation likelihood. Duration of Hormuz closure remains critical, with evidence needed to assess prolonged disruption scenario entailing significant growth reduction. G-7 rate decisions and inflation data will test stagflation lock-in under dual shocks.

regime_audit.methodology_note: Published stress_regime is the synthesiser's stress_regime_preliminary (2-domain corroboration rule). The reasoner's recommended_regime uses a stricter 3-indicator SA corroboration test and is preserved here for audit. Divergence indicates a tactical shock the synth has priced in ahead of reasoner's structural confirmation. Methodology unification under SCOPE-2026-04-17-005.

module_0.title: ECB President Lagarde warns Strait of Hormuz closure represents largest oil supply disruption in history amid relentless shock cascade
module_0.body: ECB characterizes period as relentless shock cascade including pandemic, land war, energy crisis, sweeping tariffs since 1930s, and now Hormuz closure. This energy shock compounds April 2 Liberation Day tariff package creating dual supply-side shocks. ECB baseline assumes contained conflict but adverse scenarios show prolonged disruption would significantly reduce growth and elevate inflation beyond baseline.

module_9.title: Trade and Tariff Policy
module_9.digest_note: Tariff rung 3 stable but USTR signals enforcement; national ratifications pending could trigger new retaliators
module_9.friction_analysis: EU-US Turnberry tariff deal locks in 15% rate on EU exports reducing uncertainty but eroding EU competitiveness as tariffs on rivals like China converge to similar levels. EU steel and aluminum face sustained 25% and 10% US Section 232 tariffs creating high costs for EU auto machinery construction exporters. Part of overlapping regimes with no resolution in sight despite negotiations.
module_9.tariff_escalation_rung: Tariff escalation rung remains at 3 with enacted tariffs and active EU retaliation. USTR 2026 Trade Policy Agenda doubles down on America First tariffs signaling sustained high-tariff regime without de-escalation. Risk of escalation to rung 4 via USMCA review failure with likelihood 0.5 impact 0.8.
